article_title: CISA Adds cPanel Authentication Bypass to KEV as Sorry Ransomware Crew and a Suspected Espionage Actor Hit 44,000 Servers
summary: Manual override: REJECT — article fabricates a specific quantitative claim ('110 files totalling 4.37GB stolen') and attributes it to The Hacker News, which contains no such figure. Editorial policy requires every claim to trace to a cited source; a fabricated number with named-source attribution is an unrecoverable trust violation.

findings.message: Fabricated quantitative claim attributed to The Hacker News
findings.details: The article states: 'Around 110 files totalling 4.37GB were stolen in one observed intrusion, [the same report says]' — attributing this specific figure to The Hacker News (https://thehackernews.com/2026/05/critical-cpanel-vulnerability.html). The Hacker News snapshot (source-0.html.gz) was read in full and contains NO mention of '110 files', '4.37GB', or any quantitative figure for the exfiltration. The actual Hacker News text only says: 'exfiltrate a substantial corpus of Chinese railway-sector documents' — generic language with no count or size. The 110/4.37GB figure does not appear in any of the 5 cited sources.

findings.message: Source unreachable via automated fetch (URL contains a typo: 'critrical' instead of 'critical')
findings.details: https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/critrical-cpanel-flaw-mass-exploited-in-sorry-ransomware-attacks/ — the typo is BleepingComputer's own URL slug; manual WebFetch confirmed the article exists and the cited claims (44,000 IPs, Sorry ransomware mechanics, Rivitna quote, late February exploitation) are accurate. Not a content issue, just a fetch artifact.

The article attributes the patched-build list (11.110.0.97, 11.118.0.63, 11.126.0.54, 11.132.0.29, 11.134.0.20, 11.136.0.5) to SecurityWeek. SecurityWeek actually lists nine versions (also includes 11.86.0.41, 11.124.0.35, 11.130.0.19); the six-version list cited in the article matches the NVD record's description text instead. Minor — content is accurate to NVD, just attributed to the wrong outlet.

content_preview.title: CISA Adds cPanel Authentication Bypass to KEV as Sorry Ransomware Crew and a Suspected Espionage Actor Hit 44,000 Servers
content_preview.summary: CVE-2026-41940, a 9.8-rated authentication bypass exploited as a zero-day since late February, is now driving Sorry ransomware infections and a separate espionage campaign against South-East Asian government targets.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A critical authentication bypass in cPanel and WebHost Manager (WHM), tracked as CVE-2026-41940, has moved from quiet zero-day abuse into open mass exploitation. cPanel released emergency patches on April 28, 2026, according to [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/cpanel-authentication-bypass-vulnerability-cve-2026-41940-exploited/). Two days later, on April 30, 2026, the U.S. Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency added the bug to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalo...

recommendations: REJECT: do not merge. Fabricated quantitative claim attributed to a named source is an unrecoverable factual hallucination, not a minor warning addressable via the corrections mechanism.

editor_notes.content_quality: On surface a well-structured 794-word News piece on a critical cPanel vulnerability. Subsections (vulnerability, affected versions, zero-day window, scale, ransomware, espionage activity) are sensibly organised and the tone is appropriately neutral. The quality is undermined by a fabricated quantitative claim — see source_verification.
editor_notes.source_verification:
Read all 5 source snapshots from disk; one (BleepingComputer) failed automated fetch due to a typo in BleepingComputer's own URL slug ('critrical') — verified via manual WebFetch as fallback per snapshot policy.

• source-0.html.gz (The Hacker News, Ravie Lakshmanan, 2026-05-04): Confirms Ctrl-Alt-Intel detection on May 2, 2026; *.mil.ph and *.gov.la targeting; MSP geography (Philippines, Laos, Canada, South Africa, U.S.); IP 95.111.250[.]175; AdaptixC2 framework; OpenVPN/Ligolo persistence; 'systemd persistence' detail; Censys note about Mirai variants and Sorry ransomware deployment within 24 hours of disclosure; Shadowserver 44,000-IP figure with the additional detail (omitted from article) that the figure dropped to 3,540 by May 3. CRITICAL: the snapshot contains NO quantitative figure for the exfiltration — only the generic 'substantial corpus of Chinese railway-sector documents' phrasing. The article's '110 files totalling 4.37GB' attribution to this source is fabricated.

• source-1 (BleepingComputer): URL typo blocked automated fetch; manual WebFetch confirmed the article exists and verifies: 44,000 IPs (Shadowserver), Sorry ransomware as Go-based Linux encryptor with .sorry extension, ChaCha20 stream cipher with embedded RSA-2048 public key, Tox contact, Rivitna quote 'Decryption is impossible without an RSA-2048 private key', and late-February exploitation. All claims attributed to BleepingComputer in the article are supported.

• source-2.html.gz (SecurityWeek): Confirms 'over 40,000' compromised servers; geographic top-3 (US, France, Netherlands); Rapid7's ~1.5M internet-exposed instances figure; watchTowr writeup driving exploitation spike; late-February exploitation start; 'special characters in authorization headers to write parameters to a session file' technique. Confirms 'after version 11.40' affected scope. Lists patched versions as nine builds (11.86.0.41, 11.110.0.97, 11.118.0.63, 11.124.0.35, 11.126.0.54, 11.130.0.19, 11.132.0.29, 11.134.0.20, 11.136.0.5) plus WP Squared 136.1.7 — the article's six-build list is shorter than what SecurityWeek actually lists. The shorter six-build list does match the NVD record's description text, indicating the article quoted NVD's list but attributed it to SecurityWeek.

• source-3.html.gz (CyberScoop, Greg Otto, 2026-04-30): Confirms patch released 'Tuesday' (= April 28), KnownHost zero-day quote, 'after version 11.40' scope, 'WP Squared' inclusion, session-file-write-before-verifying mechanism. Does not contradict article's April 28 patch date (Tuesday before April 30 publication).

• source-4.html.gz (NVD CVE-2026-41940): Confirms CVSS 3.1 base 9.8 (Critical), CVSS 4.0 base 9.3 (Critical), CWE-306 'Missing Authentication for Critical Function', VulnCheck as CNA, NVD published date 04/29/2026, KEV due date 05/03/2026.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: The fabricated '110 files totalling 4.37GB' figure is the breaking issue. Editorial policy ('Every claim must trace to a cited source'; 'If unsure about a fact, omit it') makes this an unrecoverable hallucination — the kind of fabricated specific that erodes trust most. A correction notice cannot retroactively make a fabricated number cited verbatim against a named outlet acceptable. The remaining attribution issue (six-build vs nine-build patched list) is recoverable but secondary.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. Tone is not at issue.
editor_notes.originality: Searched articles archive for 'cpanel', 'CVE-2026-41940', 'sorry ransomware' — no prior coverage. Topic is genuinely new.
concerns: Fabricated quantitative claim ('110 files totalling 4.37GB') attributed to The Hacker News, which contains no such figure
Misattributed patched-version list to SecurityWeek (the cited list matches NVD instead)
Some Hacker News context omitted that would have strengthened the piece — notably that Shadowserver's 44,000 figure dropped to 3,540 by May 3 (a key indicator the wave is being patched)
recommendations: Reject and discard. Per v3.9.0 editorial policy, fabricated factual claims are an unrecoverable trust violation, not a minor warning addressable via corrections.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: REJECT. A fabricated quantitative claim with named-source attribution is exactly the kind of hallucination editorial review is meant to catch. The remainder of the article is mostly accurate, but a single fabrication of this kind is sufficient to fail the trust standard. Per the maintainer's directive, anything not approved is discarded.
